text: The camera shows a fibroid uterus with multiple visible fibroids of varying sizes.
The surgeon begins the procedure by excising the right fallopian tube, with the adjacent ovary appearing normal in color and structure.
The surgeon uses a robotic device to seal and divide the ligaments and structures holding the uterus in place, carefully avoiding harm to the adjacent bladder and ureter.

The surgeon carefully retracts the bladder downward and away from the surgical field to ensure safety during the procedure. The uterus appears enlarged and heavy due to multiple fibroids, exerting pressure on the bladder.
The surgeon uses an instrument to push away translucent tissue over a small hump, indicating that the tissue is safe to manipulate.
The surgeon uses a vaginal cup placed around the cervix to elevate it, creating a visible hump that helps push the cervix away from the bladder, minimizing the risk of injury during the hysterectomy.
The surgeon carefully controls the blood vessels supplying the fibroid uterus, which are visibly prominent due to the increased vascularization caused by the fibroids.
The surgeon ensures the bladder is pushed down and away from the surgical field, extending the maneuver to the patient's left side after previously operating on the right side.

The surgeon performs skeletonization by pushing tissue away from the vaginal cup, ensuring the ureter is moved out of the operative field to prevent injury. Hidden blood vessels are also visible in the area.

The surgeon transitions to the left side of the surgical field, where the fallopian tube has already been removed in a previous procedure. The left side is prepared for further dissection as part of the hysterectomy.
The surgeon performs the safety maneuver of skeletonizing the blood vessel, ensuring it is isolated before applying an additional seal.

The surgeon exchanges instruments for scissors, which are used to open the top of the vagina. The green cup becomes visible, serving as a guide to prevent injury and ensure the vagina is not shortened during the procedure.
The assistant prepares to remove the uterus, fallopian tube, and cervix through the vagina using robotic assistance. A blue glove is placed to maintain an airtight seal at the vaginal opening.
The surgeon sutures the top of the vagina, creating the vaginal cuff, using a tight, dissolvable stitch to ensure secure closure.
The surgeon uses a stitch to sew across the top of the vagina, ensuring full thickness tissue is captured along with the supporting ligaments to provide optimal structural support.
The surgeon completes sewing the vaginal cuff, bringing the supporting ligaments into view. The needle is removed, marking the end of the surgery.
